metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Environmental Science 11 examines the structure and function of ecosystems — from abiotic factors and biodiversity to energy flow, matter cycles, and ecological relationships — and critically investigates the human practices that affect their sustainability. First Peoples ecological knowledge and stewardship practices are integrated throughout as essential frameworks for understanding and protecting Earth's systems.
metadata.general_description: A senior environmental science course exploring ecosystem structure and function, natural processes that maintain ecological balance, human impacts on sustainability, and the principles and practices of ecosystem stewardship and restoration.

big_ideas.statement: Complex roles and relationships contribute to diversity of ecosystems.
big_ideas.elaboration: Ecosystems are sustained by intricate webs of relationships — between producers, consumers, decomposers, and keystone species — that together create and maintain biodiversity at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels.

big_ideas.statement: Changing ecosystems are maintained by natural processes.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through succession, population dynamics, matter cycling, and energy flow, ecosystems naturally change and recover. Understanding these processes is essential to distinguishing natural variation from human-caused disruption.

big_ideas.statement: Human practices affect the sustainability of ecosystems.
big_ideas.elaboration: Human activities — from harvesting and pollution to habitat destruction and climate change — alter ecosystem processes in ways that can undermine long-term sustainability. Science and traditional knowledge both illuminate these impacts.

big_ideas.statement: Humans can play a role in stewardship and restoration of ecosystems.
big_ideas.elaboration: Sustainable resource management, restoration ecology, and Indigenous stewardship practices offer pathways to repairing damaged ecosystems and living in long-term balance with the natural world.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; make observations to identify questions; formulate multiple hypotheses.
i_can_statements: I can identify complex environmental questions about ecosystems, biodiversity, and human impacts.
I can make careful field and laboratory observations and generate testable environmental questions.
I can formulate multiple hypotheses about ecosystem dynamics and predict possible outcomes.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively select appropriate investigation methods; assess risks; use SI units and equipment; apply accuracy and precision concepts.
i_can_statements: I can design and conduct field investigations using appropriate environmental sampling methods.
I can assess safety, ethical, and environmental risks before conducting an investigation.
I can collect accurate data on abiotic factors (pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen) using appropriate equipment and SI units.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Interpret environments; apply Indigenous perspectives; seek patterns in data; construct models; draw evidence-based conclusions.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples ecological knowledge and land stewardship perspectives to enrich environmental analysis.
I can identify patterns and trends in ecological data — population dynamics, water quality, species distribution.
I can construct and interpret food webs, energy pyramids, and matter cycle diagrams.
I can draw evidence-based conclusions about ecosystem health and human impacts.

competencies.raw: Assess methods for errors; improve investigations; evaluate models; question bias; consider social and ethical implications.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the methods of an environmental investigation and identify sources of error and sampling bias.
I can assess the validity of ecological models and their assumptions.
I can question bias in environmental claims and evaluate the credibility of information sources.
I can consider the social, economic, and ethical dimensions of environmental decisions.

competencies.raw: Design projects with local and global connections; contribute to problem-solving through inquiry.
i_can_statements: I can apply environmental science concepts to design a stewardship or restoration project.
I can connect my learning to local environmental issues and propose evidence-based solutions.
I can consider Indigenous stewardship practices as effective and scientifically valid approaches to ecosystem management.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; communicate scientific ideas using appropriate language and evidence-based arguments.
i_can_statements: I can formulate and explain models of ecosystem function such as food webs and matter cycles.
I can communicate environmental science concepts clearly using appropriate vocabulary.
I can present evidence-based arguments about ecosystem health and environmental policy.

areas.name: Abiotic Factors and Ecosystem Characteristics
topics: Aquatic abiotic factors: pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, salinity, temperature, flow rate
Atmospheric abiotic factors: sunlight, wind, temperature, precipitation, air pressure
Edaphic (soil) factors: soil composition, topography, nutrient availability, moisture
Biomes and ecozones of BC: coastal, boreal, grassland, alpine, and freshwater

areas.name: Ecosystem Structure and Biodiversity
areas.raw: Complex roles and relationships contributing to ecosystem diversity.
topics: Levels of biodiversity: genetic, species, and ecosystem biodiversity
Ecological roles: niches, autotrophs (producers), heterotrophs (consumers), decomposers
Keystone species: disproportionate impact on ecosystem structure
Ecological relationships: predation, competition, mutualism, commensalism, parasitism
Population dynamics: carrying capacity, limiting factors, cyclic fluctuations (boom-bust cycles)
Energy flow: food chains, food webs, trophic levels, and energy pyramids (10% rule)
First Peoples knowledge of species relationships and ecosystem stewardship

areas.name: Natural Processes and Ecosystem Change
areas.raw: Changing ecosystems maintained by natural processes.
topics: Photosynthesis and cellular respiration: energy capture and release in ecosystems
Matter cycles: water, carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus cycles
Primary and secondary succession: pioneer species, climax communities, disturbance regimes
Fire ecology: role of fire as a natural disturbance in BC ecosystems
Biodiversity hotspots and refugia: centres of evolutionary diversity

areas.name: Human Impacts on Ecosystems
areas.raw: Human practices affecting sustainability of ecosystems.
topics: Resource harvesting: overfishing, clear-cutting, agriculture, mining
Pollution: point source and non-point source; water, air, and soil pollution
Invasive species: introduction pathways, ecological impacts, and control
Habitat loss and fragmentation: urban expansion, roads, and deforestation
Climate change effects on ecosystems: range shifts, phenology changes, coral bleaching
Bioaccumulation and biomagnification: toxins in food webs

areas.name: Stewardship and Ecosystem Restoration
areas.raw: Humans playing a role in stewardship and restoration of ecosystems.
topics: Principles of sustainable resource management: precautionary principle, ecosystem-based management
Ecosystem restoration: rewilding, reforestation, wetland restoration, stream rehabilitation
Indigenous stewardship practices: cultural keystone species, seasonal burning, marine stewardship
Protected areas: national/provincial parks, ecological reserves, marine protected areas
Environmental assessment and monitoring: measuring ecosystem health over time
Local restoration projects: case studies from BC ecosystems
